skill.name: prototype
skill.description: Build a throwaway prototype to flush out a design before committing to it. Routes between two branches — a runnable terminal app for state/business-logic questions, or several radically different UI variations toggleable from one route. Use when the user wants to prototype, sanity-check a data model or state machine, mock up a UI, explore design options, or says "prototype this", "let me play with it", "try a few designs".
skill.summary: Build throwaway prototypes to validate design decisions before committing to implementation.
skill.author: mattpocock

security_audit.summary: All 61 static findings are false positives. The skill uses backtick syntax exclusively for JavaScript template literals in code examples, ANSI escape codes for terminal formatting, and environment variable checks for production gating — all legitimate patterns. No malicious intent detected. Risk level confirmed as safe.

content.user_title: Build throwaway prototypes to validate design decisions
content.value_statement: Design decisions made on paper often fail when implemented. This skill helps you build quick throwaway prototypes that answer specific questions about logic or UI before committing to code.
actual_capabilities: Build interactive terminal apps to test state machines and business logic
Generate multiple UI variations on a single route with a switcher bar
Create portable logic modules that can be lifted into production after testing
Support both logic and UI prototyping branches based on the question type
Provide one-command startup using existing project task runners
Hide prototypes in production builds via NODE_ENV checks
limitations: Prototypes are explicitly throwaway with no tests or error handling
Cannot handle questions that require real database connections
UI prototypes only support read-only views by default

use_cases.title: Validate a state machine before writing it
use_cases.description: Build a tiny terminal app that drives your state model through edge cases. Watch the state change on screen and catch bugs before they reach production.
use_cases.target_user: Backend developers working on complex state transitions

use_cases.title: Explore UI layout options for a page
use_cases.description: Generate three radically different UI variations on an existing page. Flip between them in the browser to see which layout feels right before writing code.
use_cases.target_user: Frontend developers designing new pages or features

use_cases.title: Sanity-check a data model against real cases
use_cases.description: Create an in-memory prototype that tests whether your data model can represent the cases that keep appearing in your codebase. Iterate fast, delete when done.
use_cases.target_user: Architects and tech leads validating design decisions

prompt_templates.title: Prototype a state machine
prompt_templates.prompt: Prototype this state machine: [describe the states, transitions, and what edge case you want to test]. Build it as a terminal app that I can drive by hand.
prompt_templates.scenario: When you need to validate business logic before writing code

prompt_templates.title: Prototype a UI page
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to prototype the [page name] page. Generate 3 radically different layouts that I can switch between with a URL param. Show me how to run it.
prompt_templates.scenario: When you want to explore visual options before committing to one

prompt_templates.title: Test a data model
prompt_templates.prompt: Does my data model support [specific case]? Prototype it in memory so I can push it through the scenarios that keep causing bugs.
prompt_templates.scenario: When you need to validate data shapes against real-world patterns

prompt_templates.title: Compare UI approaches
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me three structurally different approaches to [describe what the page should do]. I want to switch between them and pick the one that fits best.
prompt_templates.scenario: When you need to compare architectural approaches to UI

output_examples.input: Prototype this checkout flow. I want to see if my state machine handles the case where payment fails after inventory is reserved.
output_examples.output: A terminal app appears with a command prompt. You can add items, proceed to checkout, simulate payment failures, and see the state update on screen after each action. The key insight is what happens to reserved inventory when payment fails.

output_examples.input: Prototype the settings page. I want to see a few different layouts before we commit.
output_examples.output: A settings page with three variants accessible via ?variant=A, B, or C. A floating bar at the bottom lets you switch between them. Variant A uses a two-column layout, B uses a single column with sections, C uses a card-based approach.

output_examples.input: Does our user model support delegated access? I keep finding cases where we need to act on behalf of another user.
output_examples.output: An in-memory prototype that creates users with delegation relationships and runs through the scenarios that keep appearing in bugs. You see immediately which cases the model handles and which it cannot represent.

best_practices: State the question explicitly before building. A prototype that answers the wrong question is pure waste.
Keep the logic module pure with no terminal code or I/O so it can be lifted into production later.
Delete the prototype when done. The answer is what matters, not the throwaway code.
anti_patterns: Building a prototype that needs tests — if it needs tests, it is not a prototype.
Wiring to a real database — use in-memory state unless the question is specifically about persistence.
Promoting prototype code directly to production without rewriting under normal constraints.

faq.question: When should I use the logic branch vs the UI branch?
faq.answer: Use logic when the question is about state transitions, business rules, or data shapes. Use UI when the question is about what something should look like.

faq.question: How long should a prototype live?
faq.answer: Until it answers the question. Then capture the answer and delete the code. Do not let prototypes rot in the repo.

faq.question: Can I keep the logic module from a prototype?
faq.answer: Yes, if you kept it pure with no terminal code. A reducer, state machine, or set of pure functions can be lifted into the real codebase.

faq.question: What if my project has no task runner?
faq.answer: Put the run command at the top of the prototype README. The goal is one command to start it without thinking.

faq.question: Should I add tests to a prototype?
faq.answer: No. A prototype that needs tests is no longer a prototype. It is a spike, and spikes have different rules.

faq.question: How do I handle auth in a UI prototype?
faq.answer: Keep existing auth from the host page. Only the rendering changes per variant. Do not add new auth to a prototype.
